old_claim.note: Cloud Computing & Distributed Systems provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct Search Engines to Cloud Computing edge remains because the scoped search-engine node is anchored to 1993-1994 web search systems that ran as early web services before cloud computing became a service model.

source_shape.source_locator: W3 Catalog History says W3 Catalog ran from September 2, 1993 to November 8, 1996, and describes implementation as Perl/Plexus/CGI-style web server search over mirrored resource lists.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source anchors early web search to ordinary early-Web server scripts and searchable catalogs, not cloud service infrastructure.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A 1993 web search catalog implemented with early web server scripts can establish the search-engine node without cloud computing as an upstream dependency.

ontology_before: Search Engines depended directly on Cloud Computing & Distributed Systems, conflating later scaling infrastructure with the initial web-search technology.
ontology_after: Search Engines are scoped to early web catalogs and crawler/indexer systems; cloud and distributed systems can remain later scaling context for large deployments.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: removed a later/scaling infrastructure edge. Preserved: the node still depends on the World Wide Web and algorithmic query/indexing methods.
would_reject_if: The Search Engines node is narrowed to cloud-hosted commercial search platforms only.
A source shows 1993-1994 web search systems required cloud computing as a service model.
A new large_scale_cloud_search node is introduced and intentionally depends on cloud computing.
short_reason: Cloud computing is later scaling infrastructure, not a prerequisite for early web search engines.
